“My dear Algy, you talk exactly as if you were a dentist. It is very vulgar to talk like a dentist when one isn’t a dentist. It produces a false impression—”
“Well, that is exactly what dentists do!” interrupted Algernon, leaning across the table. “And besides, I have just received a telegram—a most urgent telegram—from poor Bunbury!”
“Is he worse?” Jack asked, his brow furrowed.
“Worse? He is dead!”
“Dead?”
“Quite dead! Or, at least, he will be by tonight. I have just told the man, ‘You must tell Mr. Worthing that I am dead—stone dead—and cannot possibly come to dinner!’ and then he had the audacity to ask, ‘Shall I say you died of a chill, sir?’—as if one could choose!”
“But my dear fellow,” Jack exclaimed, “you can’t possibly ‘die of a chill’ to get out of a dinner engagement! It’s—it’s monstrous!”
“It is perfectly Bunburyist!” retried Algernon. “And if you don’t take care, your friend ‘Cecily’—who you claim is your aunt but is clearly your ward—will find you out!”
